segments.title: Roman Republican cavalry was not simply ineffective
segments.summary: A short correction to the familiar claim that Roman cavalry was inherently poor: the Republican arm was capable before Rome leaned more heavily on allied and auxiliary horsemen.
segments.body: The game discussion opens into a useful distinction between the Roman Republic and later Roman practice. Dr. Clarke argues that Republican cavalry was not automatically ineffective, but Rome's increasing reliance on other peoples for mounted formations helped create that later reputation. It is a reminder to separate a long-running military stereotype from the changing organisation of Roman forces.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts capable Republican cavalry with Rome's later reliance on other providers of cavalry formations.

segments.title: Ancient cities were not uniformly stone-grey
segments.summary: A visual-history reminder that Carthaginian and other ancient buildings often used painted colour rather than the bare-grey look familiar from ruins and games.
segments.body: Discussing the game's cityscape, Dr. Clarke notes that Carthaginians did not inhabit uniformly grey buildings. He extends the point to much of antiquity, where colour was applied to structures that now often survive as bare stone. The moment is useful when judging how historical settings are visually reconstructed.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke says Carthaginian buildings were painted with colour, as were many structures in antiquity.

segments.title: Damaging approaches to hinder a siege
segments.summary: A fortified city's approaches could be disrupted to make it harder to bring a battering ram or other siege equipment to the gate.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke points to the game depiction of defenders tearing up a road outside a gate, a measure intended to obstruct an attacking ram. He notes that comparable expedients were attempted historically, while also cautioning that they were not always sensible. The example highlights how access routes outside the wall could matter as much as the wall itself in siege defence.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains that removing the road outside a gate could impede a ram, but says the measure was not always sensible.
